verses.verse_text: Praise be to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds,
verses.commentary: The Fātiḥah possesses attributes which other sūrahs do not possess. It is said that the entire Qur'an is contained in it. It consists of twenty-five words which embrace all the sciences of the Qur'an. Part of its distinction is that Allah has divided it between Himself and His slaves, and the prayer is only valid with it, and no action is necessary to gain a reward for it. It contains tawḥīd, judgments and admonition. The word 'praise (ḥamd)' in the Arabic language means comprehensive praise. The definite article is used since the category includes all forms of praise. Allah deserves all praise since He has the Most Beautiful Names and Sublime Attributes. Praise is the opposite of blame. Taḥmīd from Form II is more extensive than ḥamd. Praise is more universal than gratitude. Allah Almighty praised Himself and began His Book with praise of Himself and did not allow that for anyone but Himself. The meaning of 'Praise be to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds' is 'My prior praise of Myself before any created being praises Me.' No matter how much His slaves try, they are unable to praise Him sufficiently, as the Master of the Messengers displayed inability when he said, 'I cannot number Your praises.' 'The Lord of all the Worlds' — 'the Lord' is their Master. Every kingdom has its lord and the Lord is the Master (mālik). The word 'Rabb' means 'master' as demonstrated by 'Mention me when you are with your lord' (12:42). The Lord is the One Who puts things right, manages, compels and preserves. Some scholars say that Rabb is the greatest name of Allah because of the large number of those who make supplication using it. There is great disagreement among interpreters about the meaning of the word 'worlds ('ālamīn)'. Qaṭādah said that it is the plural of 'ālam, and means every existent thing except Allah. The first view — that it includes every creature and existent thing — is the soundest, because evidence for it is found in 'Pharaoh said, "What is the Lord of all the worlds?" He said, "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and everything between them"' (26:23-24). The word is derived from 'alam (sign) because it indicates the One Who brought it into existence.
phrase_analysis: Praise be to Allah (al-ḥamdu lillāh) — The word 'praise' is used for the qualities of a person praised without the need of any prior act of charity on that person's part. Thanks is praise for the one thanked because of some good that he has done. Praise is more universal than gratitude because it comprises both praising and thanking. Thanks is particular, being directed towards someone who did good to you. 'Praise' is also used with approval: 'I tested him and approved (ḥamadtu) of him.' Ja'far aṣ-Ṣādiq said that ḥamd is made up of the letters ḥā', mīm, and dāl: ḥā' is from oneness (waḥdāniyyah), mīm is from dominion (mulk), and dāl is everlastingness (daymūmiyah).

the Lord (ar-Rabb) — There is disagreement about the derivation of the word rabb. Some say that it is derived from tarbiyah (upbringing), so Allah Almighty manages His creation and nurtures them, as borne out in 'Your foster daughters in your care' (4:23). On the basis that Allah manages His creation and nurtures it, Rabb reflects an attribute connected to Divine Action; in as far as Rabb means king and master, it is an attribute of the Divine Essence. When the definite article is affixed to 'Lord' on its own it can only mean Allah Almighty because it is defined, but if it is elided, it can be shared between Allah and His slaves.

of all the worlds ('ālamīn) — Qaṭādah said it is the plural of 'ālam, and means every existent thing except Allah. It has no singular form, like raht (group) and qawm (people). Ibn 'Abbās said that 'ālamīn means the jinn and human beings. Al-Farrā' and Abū 'Ubaydah said the word 'world ('ālam)' designates all who have understanding: mankind, jinn, angels and shayṭāns. Zayd ibn Aslam said they are those who are provided for. Wahb ibn Munabbih said Allah has eighteen thousand worlds. Abū Sa'īd al-Khudrī said Allah has forty thousand worlds. The word is derived from 'alam (sign) and 'alamah (token) because it indicates the One Who brought it into existence.
historical_context: At-Tirmidhī related from Ubayy ibn Ka'b that the Messenger of Allah said, 'Allah has not revealed anything like the Mother of the Book either in the Torah or the Gospel. It is the Seven Mathānī (Oft-repeated). It is divided between Me and My slave, and My slave has what he asks for.'

Al-Bukhārī transmitted from Abū Sa'īd ibn al-Mu'allā: 'I was praying and the Messenger of Allah called me but I did not answer until I had finished praying. Then he said to me, "I will teach you a sūrah which is the greatest of the sūrahs in the Qur'an before you leave the mosque." He took my hand and said, "Praise be to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds, it is the Seven Oft-Repeated ones and the Immense Qur'an that I have been given."'
Ibn al-Anbārī mentioned from Mujāhid that Iblīs lamented four times: when he was cursed, when he fell from the Garden, when Muḥammad was sent, and when the Fātiḥah of the Book was revealed.

There is disagreement about whether the Fātiḥah is Makkan or Madinan. Ibn 'Abbās, Qaṭādah, Abu-l-'Āliyah and others said that it is Makkan. Abū Hurayrah, Mujāhid, 'Aṭā' ibn Yasār, az-Zuhrī and others said that it is Madinan. The first position is considered sounder because of 'We have given you the seven Oft-repeated and the Magnificent Qur'an' (15:87) from the end of al-Ḥijr, which is agreed to be a Makkan sūrah, and the prayer was made obligatory in Makkah.

Al-Bayhaqī mentioned in the Proofs of Prophethood from Abū Maysarah that the Messenger of Allah said to Khadījah about hearing a voice calling. When he told Waraqah, he was called and told: 'O Muḥammad! Say: In the Name of Allah, the All-Merciful, Most Merciful. Praise be to Allah, the Lord of the worlds … to … misguided. Say: There is no god but Allah.' Waraqah said, 'You are the one about whom 'Īsā gave the good news and you have received the like of the Nāmūs of Mūsā.'

While Jibrīl was sitting with the Prophet, he heard a crack above him. An angel descended and said, 'Good news of two lights which you have been given which no Prophet before you was given: the Fātiḥah of the Book and the Seals of Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:285-286). You will not read a single letter of them without being rewarded for it.'
scholar_citations: Scholars disagree about the relative excellence of some sūrahs and āyahs over others. Shaykh Abu-l-Ḥasan al-Ash'arī, Qāḍī Abū Bakr ibn aṭ-Ṭayyib, Abū Ḥātim Muḥammad ibn Ḥibbān as-Sibtī and a group of fuqahā' believed none are better than any other because all are the Speech of Allah. Yaḥyā ibn Yaḥyā said that it is an error to consider one part of the Qur'an better than other parts.

Those who said that there was preference included Isḥāq ibn Rāhawayh as well as other scholars and mutakallimūn. It is the position preferred by Qāḍī Abū Bakr ibn al-'Arabī and Ibn al-Ḥaṣṣār who go by the hadith of Ubayy ibn Ka'b in which the Prophet asked, 'Ubayy, which of the āyahs of the Book is the greatest?' and he replied with the Throne Verse (2:255).
Ibn al-'Arabī said, 'He says, "Allah has not revealed its like in the Torah, the Gospel or the Qur'an." When something is the best of the best, then it is the best of all.'
Abū Jaʿfar aṭ-Ṭabarī and Abu-l-ʿAbbās al-Mubarrad believed that praise and gratitude have the same meaning. Abū 'Abd ar-Raḥmān as-Sulamī related this from Ja'far aṣ-Ṣādiq and Ibn 'Aṭā'llāh.

Ibn 'Aṭā'llāh said, 'In reality, "gratitude" is specific to praise because it is one of the blessings. Some scholars say that gratitude is more general than praise because it is shown with the tongue, the limbs, and the heart whereas praise is made only with the tongue.'

Ibn 'Abbās said, '"Praise be to Allah" is the statement of every grateful person. When he sneezed, Ādam said, "Praise be to Allah." Ibrāhīm said, "Praise be to Allah Who, despite my old age, has given me Ismā'īl and Isḥāq!" (27:15) Dāwūd and Sulaymān said, "Praise be to Allah Who has favoured us over many of His slaves who are believers." (27:15)'
Al-Ḥasan said, 'There is no blessing but that the words "Praise be to Allah" are better than it.'

Shaqīq ibn Ibrāhīm said that 'Praise be to Allah' has three aspects: acknowledging the One Who gave, showing you are pleased with what He has given, and not disobeying Him as long as there is strength in your body. These are the preconditions of praise.
Al-Khalīl said, ''Alam, 'alāmah and ma'lam are what indicates a thing, so 'ālam indicates that it has a Creator and Director.'
Al-Ḥusayn ibn al-Faḍl said that 'ālamīn in 'Of all beings, do you lie with males' (26:165) means people.
It is mentioned that a man said in the presence of al-Junayd, 'Praise be to Allah.' He told him, 'Complete it as Allah did. Say: the Lord of all the worlds.'
hadith_references: At-Tirmidhī from Ubayy ibn Ka'b: 'Allah has not revealed anything like the Mother of the Book either in the Torah or the Gospel. It is the Seven Mathānī. It is divided between Me and My slave, and My slave has what he asks for.'
Al-Bukhārī from Abū Sa'īd ibn al-Mu'allā: The Prophet said, 'Praise be to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds, it is the Seven Oft-Repeated ones and the Immense Qur'an that I have been given.'

Muslim from Abū Hurayrah: The Prophet asked Ubayy, 'Which of the āyahs of the Book is the greatest?' He replied, 'Allah, there is no god but Him, the Living, the Self-Sustaining' (2:255). The Prophet struck his chest and said, 'May Allah give you joy, Abu-l-Mundhir!'
Ad-Dārimī from Abū Sa'īd al-Khudrī: The Messenger of Allah said, 'The Fātiḥah of the Book is healing for every poison.'
Muslim from Anas ibn Mālik: 'Allah is pleased with the slave who eats and praises Him for it or takes a drink and praises Him for it.'
Ibn Mājah from Anas ibn Mālik: 'When Allah gives someone a blessing and he says, "Praise be to Allah", what he says is better than what he receives.'

Nawādir al-uṣūl from Anas: 'If the entire world were to be in the hand of a man of my Community and then he said, "Praise be to Allah," his praise of Allah would be greater than everything he possessed.'

Ibn Mājah from Ibn 'Umar: 'One of the slaves of Allah said, "O Lord, praise is Yours as befits the majesty of Your face and the immensity of Your power." His two recording angels were perplexed by it. Allah said to them, "Continue to write it as My slave said it until he meets Me and I will repay him for it."'

'Alī ibn Abī Ṭālib related that the Prophet said, 'The Fātiḥah of the Book, the Throne Verse, "Allah bears witness that there is no god but Him" (3:18) and "Say: O Allah, Master of the Kingdom" (3:26): these āyahs are suspended from the Throne. There is no veil between them and Allah.'
linguistic_analysis: The word 'praise (ḥamd)' has a plural of paucity: aḥmadī. Praise is the opposite of blame. The name 'Muḥammad' is derived from it and means the one who has many praiseworthy qualities.

All seven readings and the majority agree that there is a ḍammah on the dāl of ḥamd, making it ḥamdu. Ḥamda is related from Sufyān ibn 'Uyaynah and Ru'bah ibn al-'Ajjāj. Sībuwayh says that grammatically the nominative ḥamdu has the meaning of 'I praise Allah greatly.' If someone uses the accusative ḥamda, he is saying praise is from him alone for Allah.

Rabb in aṣ-Ṣiḥāḥ: 'Rabb is one of the Names of Allah Almighty. It is only used of someone else in a relative sense.' The word can also mean 'one who tries to put a thing right and complete it.' Rabbāniyyūn (divines) are so called because of their guarding of the Revealed Books.

'Ālamīn is the plural of 'ālam and has no singular form, like raht and qawm. The word is derived from 'alam (sign) and 'alamah (token) because it indicates the One Who brought it into existence. Az-Zajjāj said the word refers to all that Allah created in this world and the Next.

Although the word 'Lord' is usually considered to be genitive (Rabbi) in this context, it can also be read as nominative (Rabbu) or accusative (Rabba). If read as accusative, it becomes the object of the praise; if read as nominative it is the beginning of a new sentence, implying 'He is the Lord of all the worlds.'
legal_rulings: Scholars disagree about the exact nature of the obligation of reciting the Fātiḥah in the prayer. Mālik and his people say that it is incumbent on the imām and anyone praying alone in every rak'ah.
Abū Ḥanīfah, ath-Thawrī and al-Awzā'ī said that if it is omitted intentionally throughout the entire prayer and something else is recited instead, that satisfies the requirements.

Ash-Shāfi'ī said in what al-Buwaytī and Aḥmad ibn Ḥanbal reported, 'No one's prayer is satisfied unless he recites the Fātiḥah of the Book in every rak'ah, be he an imām or follower, and whether the imām recites aloud or silently.'

What is sound is the position of ash-Shāfi'ī, Aḥmad, and Mālik in his final view: the Fātiḥah is incumbent in every rak'ah on everyone by the words of the Prophet: 'There is no prayer for the one who does not recite the Fātiḥah of the Book in it,' and 'If anyone prays a prayer in which he does not recite the Umm al-Qur'an, it is incomplete,' three times.

If someone finds it impossible, after his best efforts, to learn the Fātiḥah or anything of the Qur'an, he should mention Allah in place of the recitation using whatever formula he can: takbīr, 'lā ilaha illā'llāh', praise, glorification, magnification or 'lā hawla walā quwwata illā bi'llāh'.

If someone recites the prayer in Persian when he has good Arabic, it is not allowed according to the position of the majority. Abū Ḥanīfah said recitation in Persian satisfies it even if he is good in Arabic. Ibn al-Mundhir said that does not satisfy it because it is contrary to what Allah commanded.

verses.commentary: After calling Himself 'the Lord of all the worlds' Allah then describes Himself as 'the All-Merciful, Most Merciful'. Because His description as 'the Lord of all the worlds' causes fear, He follows it by 'the All-Merciful, Most Merciful' since that contains reassurance, so that His qualities induce both awe of Him and the desire for Him. This helps people in their obedience to Him. He does the same in several places in His Book. He says: 'Tell My slaves that I am the Ever-Forgiving, the Most Merciful, but also that My punishment is the painful punishment' (15:49-50). He says elsewhere: 'The Forgiver of wrong action, the Accepter of repentance, the Severe in retribution, the Possessor of abundance' (40:3). The combination of attributes of mercy and fear is a consistent pattern throughout the Qur'an. Raḥmān is general for all existence and Raḥīm is mentioned after it to make it specific to the believers in the Next World, something made explicit by His words, 'merciful to the believers' (33:43).
phrase_analysis: the All-Merciful (ar-Raḥmān) — Raḥmān is general for all existence. It is an attribute that encompasses all of creation with mercy in this world. It is a more intensive form indicating the vastness and comprehensiveness of Allah's mercy over everything.

the Most Merciful (ar-Raḥīm) — Raḥīm is mentioned after Raḥmān to make it specific to the believers in the Next World. This is made explicit by His words, 'merciful to the believers' (33:43). The particular is mentioned after the general to call attention to the specific mercy reserved for the believers.
scholar_citations: Abū 'Alī said that Abū Bakr ibn as-Sirāj transmitted from some scholars that Allah describes Himself as being the Master of everything in 'Lord of all the worlds', so Raḥmān is not a repetition but serves a different function — following severity with reassurance to encourage obedience.
hadith_references: Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim from Abū Hurayrah: The Messenger of Allah said, 'If the believers knew the punishment of Allah, no one would hope for His Garden. If the unbelievers knew the mercy of Allah, no one would despair of His Garden.'

verses.verse_text: the King of the Day of Repayment.
verses.commentary: There are four readings of this verse: mālik, malik, malk and malīk. Scholars disagree about which is the more comprehensive: malik (king) or mālik (master). Both readings are transmitted from the Prophet, Abū Bakr, and 'Umar. It is said that 'King (malik)' is more general and intensive than 'Master (mālik)' since every king is a master but not every master is a king, and because a king has authority over the property of a master so that he can only dispose of it according to the directives of the king. It is said that 'Master is more comprehensive because He is the Master of people and other creatures and so it is more far reaching.' Allah singled out the Day of Repayment because, in this world, there is contention about who has sovereignty, as is displayed in what Pharaoh, Nimrod and other tyrants did. On that Day no one will contend with Him for sovereignty and all will be humbled to Him, as He says: 'To whom does the kingdom belong today? To Allah, the One, the Conqueror' (40:16). The word 'day' is used metaphorically here for the time from when the Resurrection takes place until the time when the people of the Next World take up their respective abodes. 'Repayment (dīn)' here means requital for actions and reckoning for them as Ibn 'Abbās, Ibn Mas'ūd, Ibn Jurayj, Qaṭādah and others said.
phrase_analysis: mālik (Master) vs malik (King) — Abū Ḥātim said that mālik is more intensive when praising the Creator than malik, and malik is more intensive when praising creatures. Qāḍī Abū Bakr ibn al-'Arabī preferred mālik for three reasons: it applies to both particular and general ('master of a house' and 'master of a kingdom'); it applies to little or much; and one says 'Master of the Kingdom (mālik al-mulk)' not 'King of the Kingdom (malik al-mulk)'. Ibn al-Ḥaṣṣār said that mālik indicates property (milk) and does not necessarily entail a kingdom (mulk), so it comprises both matters and is more intensive.

the Day (yawm) — The word 'day' normally designates the time from the rising of dawn until the moment when the sun sets. It is used metaphorically here for the entire time from when the Resurrection takes place until the people of the Next World take up their abodes. The plural of yawm is ayyām. The root is awyām and there is assimilation.

of Repayment (ad-dīn) — Dīn means requital for actions and reckoning. Linguists report that dayn is debt and dīn is repayment. The term ad-Dayyān as an attribute of the Lord means 'the Repayer'. Dīn can also mean judgment, obedience, custom and affair. Tha'lab said the verb dāna is used for when a man obeys, disobeys, is exalted, is abased, and is overcome — it is one of the words with opposite meanings.
scholar_citations: Abū 'Ubaydah and al-Mubarrad held that 'King (malik)' is more general and intensive than 'Master (mālik)' since every king is a master but not every master is a king.
Abū Ḥātim said that mālik is more intensive when praising the Creator, and malik is more intensive when praising creatures. This position was chosen by Qāḍī Abū Bakr ibn al-'Arabī.
Ibn al-Ḥaṣṣār said mālik indicates property (milk) which comprises both property and kingdom, so it is more intensive and entails completeness.
Abū 'Alī said that Abū Bakr ibn as-Sirāj argued from those who preferred mālik that Allah already describes Himself as Master of everything in 'Lord of all the worlds', so mālik avoids repetition.
Ibn 'Abbās, Ibn Mas'ūd, Ibn Jurayj, and Qaṭādah said that dīn means requital for actions and reckoning for them.
hadith_references: Al-Bukhārī and Muslim from Abū Hurayrah: 'Allah will seize the earth on the Day of Rising and roll up heaven in His right hand and then say, "I am the King. Where are the kings of the earth?"'

Abū Hurayrah reported that the Prophet said, 'The most abased man in the sight of Allah is a man who calls himself "the King of Kings".'' Muslim added, 'There is no king except Allah Almighty.' Sufyān said, 'Like the Persian title Shahanshah.'
A ḥadīth states: 'The clever one is he who calls himself to account (dāna).'
linguistic_analysis: Muḥammad ibn as-Samayqa' has 'King' in the accusative. There are four readings: mālik, malik, malk and malīk. It is related from Nāfi' that there is ishbā' on the kasrah of malik and so he recited malikī.

The word mālik is an active participle from the verb malaka. The active participle in Arabic can be used to indicate something which is still to come, like 'I will hit (ḍārib) Zayd tomorrow.' The form can be used for the future: 'Master of the Day of Repayment' means He will be the Master on the Day when it comes.
If the reading malik (King) is employed, it is one of the attributes of the Divine Essence. If mālik (Master) is used, it is one of the attributes of Divine Action.
The root of yawm is awyām and there is assimilation. Sometimes they describe hardship as 'a day'.
legal_rulings: It is not permitted to give anyone other than Allah Almighty the title 'King of Kings'. The Prophet said, 'The most abased man in the sight of Allah is a man who calls himself the King of Kings.' It is, however, permitted to be described as king or master if what is intended is what is customarily understood by those terms.

verses.verse_text: You alone we worship. You alone we ask for help.
verses.commentary: With 'You alone we worship' the third person changes to the second person based on a grammatical shift (talwīn). From the beginning to this point the sūrah has been a description of Allah and praise for Him. 'We worship' means 'We obey'. Worship is obedience and humility. A smooth (mu'abbad) road is one which is easy for travellers to travel on. Al-Harawī said, 'When those subject to the Sharī'ah say this they are acknowledging Allah's Lordship and affirming the worship of Allah alone since other people worship other than Him in the form of idols and other things.' The meaning of 'ask for help' (ista'āna) is to seek aid, support and success. The object is put before the verb because of its importance — the Arabs tend to put the most important thing first. It also ensures that the created slave and worship is not put before the object of worship. So it is not permitted to say 'na'buduka' or 'nasta'īnuka', nor to say 'na'budu iyyāka' or 'nasta'īnu iyyāka', putting the verb first before the object. One follows the wording of the Qur'an.
phrase_analysis: You alone we worship (iyyāka na'budu) — The object 'iyyāka' is put before the verb for emphasis and because of its importance. A Bedouin cursed another, and when the cursed man turned away, the curser exclaimed 'You (iyyāka) from me!' and the other replied 'From you I turn' — they put the object of emphasis first. Worship ('ibādah) means obedience and humility.

You alone we ask for help (iyyāka nasta'īn) — This adds one sentence to a prior one. The meaning of 'ask for help' (ista'āna) is to seek aid, support and success. As-Sulamī related that Abū Ḥafṣ al-Farghānī said, 'Whoever recites this cannot be considered guilty of espousing either the doctrine of fatalism or that of absolute free will.'
scholar_citations: Al-Harawī said, 'When those subject to the Sharī'ah say this they are acknowledging Allah's Lordship and affirming the worship of Allah alone since other people worship other than Him in the form of idols and other things.'

As-Sulamī in al-Ḥaqā'iq related from Muḥammad ibn 'Abdullāh ibn Shādhān from Abū Ḥafṣ al-Farghānī: 'Whoever recites "You alone we worship and You alone we ask for help" cannot be considered guilty of espousing either the doctrine of fatalism or that of absolute free will.'
linguistic_analysis: The grammatical shift (talwīn/iltifāt): From the beginning to this point the sūrah has been a description of Allah in the third person. 'You alone we worship' shifts to the second person. Similar examples occur in 'Their Lord will give them a pure drink' changing to 'This is your reward' (76:21-22) and the reverse in 'When some of you are on a boat, running before a fair wind' which changes to 'and then a violent squall comes upon them' (10:22).

Most reciters double the yā' in iyyāka in both places. 'Amr ibn Fā'id recited it as iyāk because he disliked the doubling of the yā' since it is heavy and because there is a kasra before it. One turns away from this reading.
Yaḥyā ibn Waththāb and al-A'mash recite 'nista'īn' (with kasra on the nūn). That is the dialect of the tribes of Tamīm, Asad, Qays and Rabī'ah.

verses.verse_text: Guide us on the Straight Path,
verses.commentary: The words 'guide us' are a supplication, implying that this is something desired by the speaker from their Lord. It means: 'Direct us to the Straight Path and guide us to it. Show us Your guidance which will lead us to intimacy with You and nearness to You.' Some scholars say that Allah made this sūrah a model for all supplication. Half of it comprises His praise and half comprises our needs. The supplication which is in the sūrah is the best which one can use because these are words spoken by the Lord of the worlds — when you use it you make supplication using His words which He spoke. It is said that it means: 'Guide us to follow the Sunnah in performing all the obligations we owe you.' The root of the word 'guide' is inclination towards something, as in 'We have truly turned (hudnā) to you' (7:156), meaning 'we have inclined towards You.'
phrase_analysis: Guide us (ihdinā) — It is said that the root of the word 'guide' is inclination towards something, as in 'We have truly turned (hudnā) to you' (7:156). Another facet of its meaning can be gleaned from the word hadiyah (gift) because it moves from one owner to another. Yet another aspect from the word hady, the term for an animal driven to the Ḥaram. The meaning is thus: 'Incline our hearts to the Truth.'

on the Straight Path (aṣ-ṣirāṭ al-mustaqīm) — The root meaning of ṣirāṭ in Arabic is 'a way'. An-Naqqāsh said that ṣirāṭ means road in Greek, but Ibn 'Aṭiyyah said this is very weak indeed. It is sometimes recited with a sīn rather than a ṣād, from istirāṭ meaning 'swallowing', implying the swallowing up of the Path by the one who travels on it. It is also recited as a pure zāy. Salamah related that al-Farrā' said that zirāṭ with a pure zāy is the dialect of 'Udhrah, Kalb and Banū al-Qayn. 'Straight' is an adjective meaning one which has no crookedness or deviation in it.
scholar_citations: Al-Fuḍayl ibn 'Iyāḍ said that the Straight Path is the path of the ḥajj. This is a very specific meaning and a general meaning is far more likely.
Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥanafiyyah said about 'Guide us to the Straight Path' that it means to the dīn of Allah and that no other kind of worship is accepted.

'Āṣim al-Aḥwal said that Abu-l-'Āliyah said, 'The Straight Path is the Messenger of Allah and his two companions after him.' 'Āṣim said to al-Ḥusayn that Abu-l-'Āliyah said this, and he said, 'He spoke the truth and was faithful.'
hadith_references: In a hadith: 'There is nothing Allah considers more noble than supplication.'
linguistic_analysis: Ṣirāṭ is in the accusative as a second object because the verb 'guide' is transitive, taking a second object either with the particle 'bi' or without the particle. 'Straight' is an adjective describing 'Path' and means one which has no crookedness or deviation in it, as in 'This is My Path and it is straight, so follow it' (6:153).

The word ṣirāṭ can be recited with ṣād (standard), sīn (from istirāṭ meaning 'swallowing'), between a zāy and ṣād, or as a pure zāy. Sīn is the root. Al-Farrā' said that zirāṭ with a pure zāy is the dialect of 'Udhrah, Kalb and Banū al-Qayn.

verses.verse_text: the Path of those You have blessed,
verses.commentary: This 'Path' is the same as the first and the meaning is: 'continue to guide us'. Someone may be guided to the path and then be prevented from going along it. It is also said that it is another path and means knowledge of Allah Almighty and recognition of Him, as Ja'far ibn Muḥammad said. People disagree about the identity of those who are blessed. The majority of commentators say that it means the Prophets, the people of truth, the martyrs and the righteous. They deduce that from the āyah in Sūrat an-Nisā' which says: 'Whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger will be with those whom Allah has blessed: the Prophets and the true, the martyrs and the righteous. What excellent company such people are!' (4:69). It is clear from this āyah that these are the people who have been blessed by Allah and therefore those who are referred to in the Fātiḥah as following the Straight Path. This āyah becomes a refutation of the Qadariyyah, Mu'tazilites and Shī'ah because they all believe that the will of a human being alone is enough to initiate his actions. Allah refutes them in this āyah since in it we ask Him for guidance to the Straight Path. If the matter had really been theirs and not their Lord's, they would not need to ask Him for guidance and to repeat that request in every prayer.
phrase_analysis: the Path of those You have blessed (ṣirāṭa alladhīna an'amta 'alayhim) — 'Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb and Ibn az-Zubayr recited 'ṣirāṭa man' (the path of him who) instead of 'ṣirāṭa alladhīna' (the path of those who). The 'Path' is the same as the first mentioned in verse 5. It is said to mean 'continue to guide us' since someone may be guided to the path and then be prevented from going along it. It is also said that it is another path, meaning knowledge of Allah Almighty and recognition of Him.
scholar_citations: The majority of commentators say 'those who are blessed' means the Prophets, the people of truth, the martyrs and the righteous, deduced from Sūrat an-Nisā' (4:69).
Ja'far ibn Muḥammad said that 'the Path of those You have blessed' is another path meaning knowledge of Allah Almighty and recognition of Him.
linguistic_analysis: There are ten readings of the word 'alayhim: 'alayhum, 'alayhim, 'alayhimī, 'alayhimū, 'alayhumū, 'alayhumu — these are the six forms related from the imāms among the reciters. There are four other possibilities transmitted from the Arabs, but not related from the reciters.

verses.verse_text: not of those with anger on them, nor of the misguided.
verses.commentary: There is disagreement about the identity of 'those with anger on them' and the 'misguided'. The majority say that 'those with anger on them' are the Jews and 'the misguided' are the Christians. That was explained by the Prophet in the hadith of 'Adī ibn Ḥātim and the story of how he became Muslim, transmitted by Abū Dāwūd aṭ-Ṭayālisī and at-Tirmidhī. That explanation is also attested to by the Almighty who says about the Jews: 'They brought down anger from Allah on themselves' (2:61, 3:112) and 'Allah is angry with them' (48:6). He says about the Christians that they 'were misguided previously and have misguided many others, and are far from the right way' (5:77). It is also said that 'those with anger on them' are the idolaters and that the misguided are the hypocrites. The word 'misguidance' (ḍalāl) in Arabic means 'missing the target and straying from the path of Truth.' Regarding saying 'Āmīn' after this verse: it is sunnah for the reciter of the Qur'an to say 'Āmīn' when he finishes the Fātiḥah, after a moment of silence following the nūn of 'ḍāllīn' to distinguish what is part of the Qur'an from what is not. The meaning of āmīn according to the people of knowledge is: 'O Allah, give us an answer!' Al-Jawharī said it means 'Let it be like that.' It is said to strengthen the supplication and ask for the descent of blessing.
phrase_analysis: not of those with anger on them (ghayri-l-maghḍūbi 'alayhim) — 'On them' is in the nominative because it means 'anger is on them.' Linguistically 'ghaḍab' (anger) means intensity of feeling. The meaning of anger when attributed to Allah is the will to punish — an attribute of the Essence since the will of Allah is one of the attributes of the Essence. It is also said to mean the punishment itself, corroborated by the ḥadīth: 'Ṣadaqah extinguishes the anger of the Lord.'

nor of the misguided (walā aḍ-ḍāllīn) — The word 'misguidance' (ḍalāl) in Arabic means 'missing the target and straying from the path of Truth.' One way the word is used is to describe when milk dissolves (ḍalla) in water. It is also used in 'When we have been absorbed (ḍalalnā) into the earth' (32:10). There is disagreement about the 'lā' in 'nor (walā)': it is said to be redundant (aṭ-Ṭabarī), for stress so no one supposes 'misguided' is added to 'those' (Makkī and al-Mahdawī), or it means 'ghayr' (the Kufans).
historical_context: The Prophet explained the identity of 'those with anger on them' and 'the misguided' in the hadith of 'Adī ibn Ḥātim and the story of how he became Muslim, transmitted by Abū Dāwūd aṭ-Ṭayālisī in his Musnad and at-Tirmidhī in his Collection.
scholar_citations: The majority say that 'those with anger on them' are the Jews and 'the misguided' are the Christians, explained by the Prophet in the hadith of 'Adī ibn Ḥātim.

Al-Muhallab said, 'The locus of the charm is in the words "You alone we worship and You alone we ask for help."' It is also said that the entire sūrah is a charm based on the words of the Prophet to the man who recited it as a charm for someone with a snake-bite: 'What taught you that it is a charm?'
'Aṭā' said that āmīn is a supplication. Ibn az-Zubayr and those behind him said it until the mosque reverberated.

At-Tirmidhī said, 'That is the position of more than one of the people of knowledge among the Companions: a man should raise his voice with āmīn and not say it silently.' Ash-Shāfi'ī, Aḥmad and Isḥāq said that.

The people of Abū Ḥanīfah said that it is more appropriate to say āmīn silently rather than out loud because it is supplication and Allah Almighty says, 'Call on your Lord humbly and secretly' (7:55).
Ibn al-Qāsim related from Mālik that the imām does not say āmīn but those behind him do. That is the position of Ibn al-Qāsim and the Egyptians among Mālik's followers.

The first position (saying it aloud) is sound because of the hadith of Wā'il ibn Hujr who said, 'When the Messenger of Allah recited "nor of the misguided," he said "Āmīn," raising his voice.' Al-Bukhārī has a chapter on 'The imām saying āmīn out loud.'
Wahb ibn Munabbih said, 'Āmīn consists of four letters. Allah creates an angel from every letter which says, "O Allah, forgive the one who said āmīn!"'
Some people say that āmīn is one of the Names of Allah. That is related from Ja'far ibn Muḥammad, Mujāhid and Hilāl ibn Yasāf.

Al-Jawharī said there are two ways of pronouncing āmīn: with both vowels long like Yāsīn, and with the first vowel short like yamīn. Doubling the mīm is an error, although the doubling is related from al-Ḥasan and Ja'far aṣ-Ṣādiq.

As-Sulamī said in al-Ḥaqā'iq and al-Māwardī in his commentary that the view that 'those with anger on them' refers to people who omit reciting the Fātiḥah in prayer is nonsense. Al-Māwardī said this view is rejected because reports contradict it.
hadith_references: At-Tirmidhī from Abū Dāwūd aṭ-Ṭayālisī from the hadith of 'Adī ibn Ḥātim: The Prophet explained that 'those with anger on them' are the Jews and 'the misguided' are the Christians.
Abū Hurayrah reported that the Messenger of Allah said, 'When the imām says "Āmīn," say "Āmīn." If someone's āmīn coincides with that of the angels, he will be forgiven his past wrong actions.'
Wā'il ibn Hujr said, 'When the Messenger of Allah recited "nor of the misguided," he said "Āmīn," raising his voice.' Abū Dāwūd and ad-Dāraquṭnī transmitted it.

Abū Dāwūd related that Abū Muṣabbaḥ al-Maqrānī said they used to sit with Abū Zuhayr an-Namīrī, one of the Companions, who related: 'One night we went out with the Messenger of Allah and came to a man who was intense in asking. The Prophet stood listening and said, "It is guaranteed if he seals it." When asked with what, he answered, "With āmīn."'
In a report: 'Jibrīl taught me āmīn when I finished the Fātiḥah of the Book and said that it is like a seal on the Book.'
In another hadith: 'Āmīn is the seal of the Lord of the worlds.'
In another hadith: 'Āmīn is a degree in the Garden.'

Muslim from Abū Mūsā al-Ash'arī: 'The Messenger of Allah addressed us and taught us our prayer. He said, "When he says 'not of those with anger on them nor of the misguided,' say āmīn and Allah will answer you."'

At-Tirmidhī al-Ḥakīm from Anas ibn Mālik: 'Allah has given three things to my Community that He did not give to anyone before them: the greeting of the people, the rows of the angels, and āmīn, except for what was said by Mūsā and Hārūn.'
Ibn Mājah from 'Ā'ishah: 'The Jews do not envy you for anything as they envy you for the salām and saying āmīn.'
Ibn 'Abbās reported: 'The Jews do not envy you for anything as much as they envy you for the āmīn. So say āmīn often.'
Abū Sa'īd al-Khudrī reported that the Prophet said to a man who had recited the Fātiḥah as a charm for someone with a snake-bite, 'What taught you that it was a charm?'
A ḥadīth states: 'Ṣadaqah extinguishes the anger of the Lord.'
linguistic_analysis: 'Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb and Ubayy ibn Ka'b inserted a second ghayri before ḍāllīn. It is related from the two of them with the rā' in the accusative and genitive on both letters. If it is in the genitive, it is an appositive for 'those' or for 'hum' in 'on them' or as an adjective of 'those' which is definite.

The word 'misguidance' (ḍalāl) in Arabic means 'missing the target and straying from the path of Truth.' One way the word is used is to describe when milk dissolves (ḍalla) in water. It is also used in 'When we have been absorbed (ḍalalnā) into the earth' (32:10). Ḍalḍalah is a smooth stone which water has turned over again and again in a valley.

There are two ways of pronouncing āmīn: with both vowels long like Yāsīn, and with the first vowel short like yamīn. Doubling the mīm is an error according to al-Jawharī. The doubling is related from al-Ḥasan and Ja'far aṣ-Ṣādiq. Al-Ḥusayn ibn al-Faḍl derived it from amma meaning to aim for something, giving it the meaning 'we aim for You,' as in 'or those heading (āmmīn) for the Sacred House' (5:2).
The root of 'ḍāllīn' is 'ḍālilīn' and the vowel of the first lām has been elided and then the lām assimilated into the other lām.

There is disagreement about the 'lā' in 'nor (walā) of the misguided'. It is said to be redundant (aṭ-Ṭabarī); for stress so no one supposes 'misguided' is added to 'those' (Makkī and al-Mahdawī); or it means 'ghayr' according to the Kufans and is the reading of 'Amr and Ubayy.
legal_rulings: It is sunnah for the reciter of the Qur'an to say 'Āmīn' when he finishes the Fātiḥah, after a moment of silence following the nūn of 'ḍāllīn' to distinguish what is part of the Qur'an from what is not part of it.

Scholars disagree about whether the imām says āmīn out loud. Ash-Shāfi'ī and Mālik in the transmission of the Madinans say it aloud, whereas the Kufans and some Madinans say it should not be said out loud. Ibn al-Qāsim related from Mālik that the imām does not say āmīn but those behind him do.
The sound position is that the imām says āmīn out loud, based on the hadith of Wā'il ibn Hujr and al-Bukhārī's chapter on 'The imām saying āmīn out loud.'

Our scholars say that the forgiveness of wrong actions when saying āmīn is dependent on four conditions: the imām saying āmīn, those with him saying āmīn, the āmīn of the angels, and the coinciding of the āmīns.
